$comment: Top-level argument graph for 'Verified Legal Reasoning: From Natural Language to Formal Proof'. Every claim either points to a lean4_ref (verified) or is marked with confidence < 1.0 (needs verification).
title: Verified Legal Reasoning: From Natural Language to Formal Proof

thesis.claim: Legal reasoning can be partially formalised in dependent type theory, and the boundary between formalisable and non-formalisable elements can itself be encoded within the type system.

components.claim: Dependent types (Lean 4) can encode legal eligibility criteria as propositions, where evidence inhabits the type.

support.from: Curry-Howard correspondence: proofs are programs, propositions are types
support.to: Legal evidence is a program (construction), legal eligibility is a type (specification)

support.source: Lawsky 2017 - default logic for statutes demonstrates feasibility of logical encoding

components.claim: Legal reasoning is non-monotonic (defeasible): new evidence or higher-priority rules can retract conclusions. This must be first-class in any formalisation.
Hart's open texture (The Concept of Law, 1961) is not an argument against formalisation but a boundary condition that can be encoded WITHIN the type system as a FormalisationBoundary.
components.note: This is the intellectually novel contribution. No prior work (DDL, Catala, L4L, LegalRuleML) treats the formalisation boundary as a first-class type.

support.objection: Open texture makes full formalisation of law impossible (Hart 1961, Schauer 2011)
support.response: We agree full formalisation is impossible. Our contribution is encoding WHERE and WHY it is impossible as machine-checkable annotations, not external commentary.

defeats.objection: Encoding the boundary is trivially adding String annotations, not a genuine formal contribution.
defeats.response: The RequiresHumanDetermination typeclass constrains the proof search: the type checker REFUSES to discharge obligations marked as Vague or Discretionary without explicit human input. This is a formal guarantee, not a comment.
defeats.note: This is the weakest link in the argument. Need to demonstrate that the typeclass actually constrains proof search in a meaningful way, not just annotates.

components.claim: A Legal Intermediate Representation (IR) can bridge the gap between natural language legal text and formal Lean 4 types, serving as the structured output of LLM transduction and the input to formal verification.

support.description: LLMs as heuristic transducers (messy input → structured IR); Lean 4 as invariant verifier (IR → verified conclusions). Clean separation of concerns.

components.claim: This approach (LegalLean) is strictly more expressive than existing formalisms (DDL, Catala, L4L, LegalRuleML) along at least 3 of 8 expressiveness dimensions.
components.note: Requires the comparison table to be filled in with actual evidence. This is a strong claim that needs careful validation against each system.

defeats.objection: Catala already handles statutory computation with formal guarantees and has a larger community.
defeats.response: Catala is a DSL for computing statutory obligations, not for reasoning about them. It cannot express defeasibility, open texture, or dependent evidence. Different tools for different jobs.

draftingOrder.rationale: Do NOT start with the literature review. Start with the IR because once the intermediate representation is canonicalised, the rest of the monograph becomes much easier to write.

tier1_must_impress.name: Sarah Lawsky
tier1_must_impress.reflection: Sees her default logic for statutes handled rigorously in Lean 4 dependent types

tier1_must_impress.name: Monica Palmirani
tier1_must_impress.reflection: Sees an isomorphic, verifiable encoding of norms with stronger guarantees

tier1_must_impress.name: Jeremy Avigad
tier1_must_impress.reflection: Sees Lean used in a new, high-stakes domain beyond mathematics

tier1_must_impress.name: Leonardo de Moura
tier1_must_impress.reflection: Sees his proof assistant applied to law — reputationally significant

tier1_must_impress.name: Guido Governatori
tier1_must_impress.reflection: Sees DDL's ideas in a dependently typed setting with machine-checkable guarantees

tier1_must_impress.name: Zhe Hou
tier1_must_impress.reflection: Sees an upgrade path from SMT to Lean 4 for legal reasoning

keyReferences.author: Sarah Lawsky
keyReferences.title: A Logic for Statutes
keyReferences.relevance: Gold standard for default logic encoding of statutes. IRC §163(h) case study baseline.

keyReferences.author: H.L.A. Hart
keyReferences.title: The Concept of Law
keyReferences.relevance: Open texture doctrine. Our FormalisationBoundary is the formal response to Hart's challenge.

keyReferences.author: Guido Governatori et al.
keyReferences.title: Computing Contracts with Defeasible Deontic Logic
keyReferences.relevance: DDL baseline for TCP Code comparison.

keyReferences.author: Denis Merigoux et al.
keyReferences.title: Catala: A Programming Language for the Law
keyReferences.relevance: Comparison system: DSL for statutory computation (different scope from our reasoning framework).

keyReferences.author: Zhe Hou et al.
keyReferences.title: L4L: Towards Trustworthy Legal AI through LLM Agents and Formal Reasoning
keyReferences.relevance: Closest competitor: LLMs + SMT. Our upgrade: LLMs + Lean 4 dependent types.

keyReferences.author: Monica Palmirani et al.
keyReferences.title: LegalRuleML Core Specification v1.0
keyReferences.relevance: XML standard for normative rules. Our Lean 4 types provide machine-checkable guarantees that XML cannot.
